title: Dog Greyish Discharge from Male Sheath: Causes and Triage Guide
meta_desc: Notice greyish discharge from your dog's sheath? Learn when it is normal (smegma) and when you should contact a vet in this quick triage guide.
content_html:
What is the Greyish Discharge?

In most cases, a small amount of grey, white, or yellowish discharge from a male dog's sheath is a substance called smegma. This is a natural lubricant produced by the body to keep the area clean and protected. It is composed of skin cells, oils, and moisture and is considered a normal biological occurrence in intact and neutered male dogs alike.

Is This an Emergency?

The urgency level for this symptom is Low. If your dog is eating, drinking, and behaving normally, greyish discharge is usually not a cause for immediate alarm. However, if the discharge changes color to green or bright red, develops a strong foul odor, or if your dog is excessively licking the area and seems in pain, you should schedule a routine appointment with your veterinarian to rule out a mild infection known as balanoposthitis.

Why a Photo Helps Triage

Providing your veterinary team with a clear, well-lit photo of the discharge and the surrounding skin can be extremely helpful. A visual assessment allows a professional to differentiate between normal smegma and signs of inflammation or infection. Seeing the exact color, consistency, and volume of the fluid helps the vet decide if your dog needs to be seen right away or if simple monitoring at home is sufficient.

faqs.question: Is smegma normal in male dogs?
faqs.answer: Yes, a small amount of clear, white, or greyish discharge is a normal biological lubricant for male dogs.

faqs.question: Should I be worried if my dog licks the area?
faqs.answer: Occasional grooming is normal, but if your dog is licking the area obsessively or if the skin looks red and irritated, it may indicate an infection that requires veterinary attention.

faqs.question: How can I tell if the discharge is an infection?
faqs.answer: Signs of infection include a change in color to green or bloody red, a foul smell, swelling of the sheath, or difficulty urinating.
